data.title: Treasury Issuance Is Creating A Liquidity Storm Beneath The Market's Surface
data.description:
The past week suggests that liquidity constraints persist and will, at some point, force the Fed to begin increasing the size of the balance sheet. But for now, those constraints show up in both overnight funding rates remaining elevated and in primary dealers' balance sheets, which are seeing significant rises in Treasury holdings.

These are all signs that suggest the recent deluge of Treasury debt has drained reserves and clogged primary dealer balance sheets. Additionally, risky assets like Bitcoin have collapsed, and the stock market has seen volatility increase while trading has been basically sideways.

Reserves Remain Low

Reserve balances at the Fed remain basically unchanged this past week, even now that quantitative tightening has completed. A sign that reserve balances are likely to stay below the $3 trillion mark for the foreseeable future or until such a time that the Fed decides to expand the balance sheet actively. Reserves will continue to ebb and flow with changes in the Treasury General Account or other liability factors, but, in total, they are unlikely to deviate much.

What is also notable is that liquidity constraints are not confined to the reserves held at the Fed. Now that the TGA is filled, reserves are no longer being drained. The pressures from debt issuance are now building on the balance sheets of primary dealers. Recently, there has been a surge in primary dealers' holdings of Treasury bills and, more generally, Treasuries. In fact, Treasury holdings have reached their highest level ever.

Together, this suggests that liquidity constraints continue to build in the market, which is why we continue to see the secured overnight financing rate trade above the Fed's effective funds rate and, more importantly, big swings and increased volatility in the overnight funding market rates.

Impacts Are Being Felt

The tightening of liquidity conditions has apparently and predictably had a meaningful impact on risk assets like Bitcoin, which is down about 29% from its October peak, with its movement loosely reflecting changes in reserve balances over time.

The equity market has been trading in a wide range, as it seems to be moving around in much broader ranges more recently, which is not surprising given that liquidity in the overnight market has tightened. In fact, in 2018, when the balance sheet shrank, the index traded sideways from January 2018 until October 2019, with just a lot of volatility in between.

During that time frame, in 2018, we saw a similar type of fluctuation between the secured overnight financing rate and the effective funds rate. It wasn't until the overnight funding rate spike in October 2019 that the Fed was forced to end QT and restart balance sheet expansion.

Arguably, the Fed has tools in place to prevent a similar occurrence to the 2019 repo rate spike, with the standing repo facility in place. But what it underscores is that market dynamics today are comparable to those back in 2018 and 2019, when the reserve balance got too low.

So while it may seem that the effects of tighter liquidity are not being felt in risk assets, the decline in Bitcoin suggests they are. Additionally, the increased volatility and sideways trading in the equity market also suggest they are being felt as well.

It would seem they may only begin to feel them when primary dealers are forced to absorb increased Treasury issuance now that the TGA has been replenished, and those impacts are likely yet to be felt.

data.title: Meta Platforms Stock (META): Is It A Buy Or Hold? | 2-Minute Analysis
data.description:
Get Started With Seeking Alpha Premium Today!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Welcome to 2 Minute Analysis. Our goal is to not only entertain, but provide value and insights about the investments you care about. Today's episode is from this comment. So, let's throw 2 minutes on the clock and dive-in.

Today, we are diving into Meta Platforms, ticker symbol, META. Kicking things off here with the Quant rating system, it currently has a Hold rating on the stock, and we're going to dive deeper into the metrics here in just a moment.

Jumping over to the Seeking Alpha Analysts, they currently have a Buy rating on the stock in aggregate, and that's 24 analysts providing coverage in the last 30-days. Lastly, Wall Street currently have a Strong Buy rating on the stock, and that's from a whopping 68 analysts providing coverage in the last 90-days.

To learn more about how the Seeking Alpha Quant system and Seeking Alpha Analysts outperform the market, visit the link in the description of this video.

Now, let's dive deeper. This is a $1.5 trillion market capitalization company in the communication services sector and interactive media and services industry.

Jumping into the Valuation grade here, it is currently an F. Now, a few notable metrics to point out here, the PEG non-GAAP forward ratio is currently 1.55, compared to the sector at 1.49, which is mainly in-line. Then if we look at the EV-to-EBITDA forward-looking metrics here, we got 12.95, compared to the sector at 8.16, showing that it's a little bit rich. And then lastly, the price-to-cash flow here of 14.79, compared to the sector at 7.41, giving it that C- grade there. So, obviously, people paying up for this company because of the growth prospects, but creating a rich valuation.

Jumping over to the Growth grade, which is currently a C+, the year-over-year revenue growth of 21.27% far surpasses the 2.86% sector median revenue growth here, which we love to see. The one thing that is worrying here is the free cash flow per share growth rate is negative 14.25% currently. Something to keep an eye on. Leverage free cash flow growth year-over-year is also negative 51%.

Alright. Jumping into Profitability, it’s currently an A+ grade. If we look at the EBITDA margin, it currently is 51.94%, compared to the sector at only 19.35%. Love to see that. And then also cash from operations is $107.57 billion. That's just incredible to see. Truly profitable company in Meta Platforms.

Momentum grade is currently a C. Now, this one's interesting. One year price performance of 5%, but more recently the three-month price performance of negative 18%. Looks like this stock has been whipsawing back and forth over the last year.

Lastly, Revisions grade C-, we have four up revisions and 45 down revisions for EPS over the last three months, and 56 upward revisions for revenue with zero down revisions for revenue over the last three months. Obviously, short-term, they're expecting more revenue coming into Meta Platforms, but maybe not the most efficient operations for shareholders that are invested here.

Now, Meta Platforms does pay a dividend. It is a very small yield of 0.34%. And if we look at the dividend grades here, dividend safety is A+, dividend growth is A+, dividend yield is D-, and dividend consistency is F. Now, dividend was just recently started back in 2024, so we need to see more time for consistency. But the safety and the growth grades should be pretty safe due from the amount of cash that this company generates.

However, if we look at the yield, it is 0.33%, compared to the sector, 3.83%. So, not really a stock for your income. However, capital appreciation may be on the horizon. We'll see what happens from here on out.

Now, that's going to wrap it up for this episode. If you have a ticker you want us to cover, add it to the comment section below, and don't forget to follow Seeking Alpha so you get notified when the next 2 Minute Analysis gets published.

data.title: Never Short A Dull Market: Crude Oil Spike Could Be 2026 Surprise
data.description:
The old Wall Street adage, especially in the commodities market, is "don't short a dull market." Why? Because things change.

Outside of precious metals gains in the second half of 2025, most other commodities have been missing in action. Many have witnessed flat to declining prices.

One sector I have been warming up to over the last month is oil/gas, especially crude oil (CL1:COM, CO1:COM). After better than three years of steady decline in pricing and investor interest, the petroleum industry could be ripe for a turnaround.

The gold-to-oil ratio is incredibly stretched, with only a few weeks during the height of the 2020 COVID pandemic economic shutdown producing a more skewed ratio between the two assets/commodities. I have drawn a graph back to 1947 of this comparison calculation, compliments of MacroTrends. Honestly, we are reaching for modern history records of cheapness for crude oil per barrel priced at 1/70th the equivalent of hard money gold value per ounce.

I wrote about this development back in October here, while this ratio has continued to expand for many months running. Crude oil in particular could be reaching for a final low in November-December, if a recession can be avoided over the next 6-12 months and/or any supply disruption event occurs in coming weeks (think Trump bombing Venezuela as one possibility).

The December 2025 setup for crude oil could also be mimicking the December 1998 instance on a variety of levels, including seasonal, cyclical, trend, and macro considerations (although important differences include weaker demand growth in a global economy hurt by U.S. tariffs, offset by a realistic chance for a major supply shock). For example, the Dotcom technology boom of the late 1990s looks to be rhyming with the current AI bubble. The December 1998 bottom appeared after two years of steady decline in oil, and followed a 20% stock market decline by roughly four months (potentially six months today, if the October 2025 low holds). Measured from its low price in late 1998, crude oil tripled over the next 16 months (from US$10 to $33 a barrel).

If you are a contrarian investor (like me), finding an industry or commodity that is legitimately unloved or misunderstood happens only several times each year. With almost nobody expecting a dramatic rally in crude oil anytime soon, trading activity in WTI futures has plummeted at the end of 2025. You could say the crude oil market has become one of the most "dull" of any commodity.

In the Commitments of Traders [COT] Report, commercial firms have almost moved to net-long in their positioning, while speculators and hedge funds have cut back their interest to almost zero net-longs. I have boxed in gold below, this strange and unusual development. [Note: data is through October 21st, a result of the federal government shutdown.]

At this stage, market players are uniformly expecting a dull to bearish crude oil drift in price to continue, with an apparent oversupply of the market forecast by outfits like the EIA. A weakening U.S. economy and labor market does not add any bullish excitement to the supply/demand balance. According to the last forecast in November, crude oil is projected to remain under price pressure from oversupply in early 2026. Bearish sentiment is clearly the "conventional wisdom" conclusion in early December.

This is where the upside opportunity comes into play. The crude oil market is notoriously cyclical, and can (does) turn without warning. For example, crude oil had a track record of rising and falling from radical OPEC changes in supply (think 1973-74 oil embargo, or intentional Saudi oversupply in 1986 and 2020, etc.) or the advent of a war hurting supply and spiking price (think 1990 Persian Gulf War - Iraq invasion of Kuwait, 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine). In economic terminology, crude oil demand is very inelastic, where large price rallies do not slow total consumer/business appetite overnight.

Well, we could be nearing another supply problem if Russia expands its war in Ukraine to surrounding nations or oil transportation networks. President Putin specifically made threats against Western oil tankers in the Black Sea this week. Plus, a new supply headache for the world could quickly appear if President Trump orders a naval blockade of Venezuelan oil supply and/or employs military force to remove President Maduro from power.

So, the dull crude oil market in the first week of December could be sitting on a powder-keg of "multiple" disruption risks in the days/weeks ahead.

Where We Stand - U.S. Supply

U.S. inventories and production are slightly above a year ago (3% and 2% respectively), but not amazingly so. Given minor economic growth, stocks to usage are actually about the same as late 2024.

Total stocks including refined products are somewhat above 2022-24 levels, but nowhere near the COVID glut situation of 2020-21 in America.

In the end, my worry is any serious supply disruption from geopolitical black-swan trouble in the world leaves the U.S. market with only "average" above-ground supplies (on top of a largely depleted federal SPR emergency reserve extracted during 2022-24, sitting near 50% of capacity).

Further, breakeven costs to bring on new production were estimated at US$47 per barrel globally by Rystad Energy consulting last year, with a projection of base costs rising to $55 by 2030. Translation: the current $59 price may not have a lot of room to fall, else depleting production rates from existing wells and oil fields may not be replaced. In turn, the absence of new wells would eventually create a major supply shortage and significant jump in prices to encourage drilling and exploration a year or two down the road.

Price Breakout Near?

I mentioned in my October article, the time to get excited about oil/gas investments could appear when crude oil once again recaptures its 50-day moving average. At that point, a catch-up phase to gold could begin. All told, assuming gold prices stay around US$4,000 an ounce, a 10-year "normalized" gold-to-oil ratio of 30x would mean crude oil may be headed to $120-$130 in quick order during 2026. That's quite a distance from $59 now.

Here's where we stand. The 50-day moving average could be breached on the upside any day. On the graphs below you can review how a price compression in trading activity inside something of a wedge/triangle pattern is also in the works. I am thinking another few dollars of upside in crude oil could generate a rather important buy signal for investing in the whole energy/petroleum sector of Wall Street, especially if a supply disruption event is part of the story. In other words, $61-62 could provide the technical trigger for capital to come rushing back into oil/gas investments, with a break of the 200-day moving average around $64 confirming a long-term recovery is emerging.

Final Thoughts

Of course, absent a geopolitical shock to supply, the appearance of recession in America may set the stage for a crude oil price drop below $55 a barrel. Then, $50-55 could prove a final low area for this critical energy commodity, before a reversal above $65 later in 2026.

We have to remember $50 in today's dollars would be about the same as $40 in early 2020, before COVID hit and money printing exploded inflation. From quotes under $40 during the summer of 2020, crude oil would move from bust to boom and $120 in the spring of 2022. A similar triple from $50 would bring $150 a barrel by 2028.

Needless to say, oil/gas exploration and development companies are not priced for a substantial increase in underlying values, assuming sales and earnings are about to gallop higher. In a sense, this group may be next in line for extraordinary gains, following the gold miner advance of 2024-25. At least, that's what reasonable investors can extrapolate from the rare gold-to-oil spread.

Another possibility for gold and oil prices to move in opposite directions and reset their traditional price relationship is gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) implode at the same time as crude oil flatlines or rises a bit. This would be the "credit crunch" scenario for financial markets and base money. I would place the odds of this outcome at less than 20% in 2026-27.

The next most likely scenario (30% odds) is gold declines back to $3500 oz. while crude oil climbs to $80-90 a barrel next year, rebalancing the gold-to-oil ratio around 40x. Yet, the greatest odds in my view favor gold remaining high (as the world's geopolitics remain in chaos), with oil surprising on the upside as it zigzags above $100 for a quote in 2026.

It's the reason why you don't short a dull commodity market, when everyone seems to have figured out an oversupply condition will persist indefinitely. Few analysts/investors are modeling an outlier supply-shock situation unfolds, where marketplace equilibrium (even in surplus) is hit rapidly by a shortage.

My baseline crude oil forecast for 2026 is any prolonged supply disruption could easily push price above $100 over 6-12 months (with a definite shot at $150 over 24 months), given the world's economy avoids a deep recession.

That's my bullish thinking and analysis of the crude oil setup in early December 2025. Food for thought anyway, if you don't mind being exposed to contrarian research.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Regencell Bioscience: Material Weaknesses Add To Woes
data.description:
Introduction

Regencell Bioscience (RGC) is a company focused on using traditional Chinese medicine to treat autism, among other conditions. It has achieved notoriety in the investing world thanks to huge rise in the stock price this year, without a clear explanation for that surge. I initiated coverage in June, rating the stock a sell and saying that the fundamentals and meme potential were both lacking. Since that piece, the stock is down roughly 75%.

Valuation

In my follow up article on Regencell, I stated:

Even after the massive drop, Regencell is still worth over $6 billion as of this writing. This is despite the fact that the company has no revenue, no upcoming clinical trials, and no meaningful assets on the balance sheet.... Regencell was overvalued at $30 billion in June; it is overvalued after an 80% drop to $6 billion now, and I believe it would still be overvalued if it fell another 80% to $1.2 billion. There isn't any particular price that is low enough to compensate for the total lack of actual business activity or assets.

This remains true. The company has not posted any meaningful news that would increase this valuation. The company has not posted any business activity, nor has any high profile financial influencer tried to make the case for the stock on fundamentals. This makes sense, as there aren't any meaningful fundamentals to speak of for this company.

Material Weaknesses Raise Red Flags

On November 1st, the company published a 20-F report. This report was rife with massive red flags. The company stated:

In the course of auditing our consolidated financial statements as of and for the years ended June 30, 2025, 2024 and 2023, we and our independent registered public accounting firms identified three material weaknesses in our internal control over financial reporting as well as other control deficiencies.

The company describes these weaknesses as related to lack of knowledge, lack of proper risk assessment protocols, and failure to segregate certain financial duties, which sounds relatively innocuous at first glance but could be red flags. The American Accounting Association has done an extensive study on these types of material weakness issues, which I suggest you read if you're interested in learning more.

These red flags do not come as a surprise to me, as Regencell has been mentioned in the Financial Times alongside other Chinese and Hong Kong-listed companies that are suspected of being involved in pump and dump scams.

New Autism Treatments Limit Identity-Based Meme Stock Potential

In my original coverage, I noted that some meme stocks, such as Trump Media (DJT) operate as a proxy for identity expression, and mentioned the fact that Regencell is offering a potential treatment for autism as potentially key there. If prominent political influencers got involved, then Regencell could have more of a base of support. However, the FDA recently approved another controversial drug to treat autism instead, with Trump's blessing. This effectively takes away one of the avenues for long-term meme potential that Regencell had.

New Price Surge has Zero Catalyst

The original massive spike in the price for Regencell has never really been explained, as there was no clear cause, legitimate or otherwise. A similar spike happened Dec 4, 2025, with the stock up 20% on no apparent news. The company has not issued any information (aside from the aforementioned 20-F report) in 6 months, and the only content to be found about the company continues to be from financial content creators speculating about the price.

The Bottom Line

Regencell remains a company with a $6 billion market cap, despite a total lack of any meaningful business activity or assets. Aside from the sharp rise in the price of the stock, there isn't much positive to discuss. Meanwhile, the 20-F raises red flags about at least three material weaknesses in their accounting, and there remains no clear path for the company to achieve any sort of long-term meme stock status.

As I mentioned in my prior update, finding a way to bet against Regencell's price is difficult due to illiquid options, which prevents me from rating the stock a Strong Sell. Still, I have to say in the strongest possible terms that this company should be avoided.

data.title: Victoria's Secret: Round Trip For The Stock, Back On Track
data.description:
Victoria's Secret & Co. (VSCO) is a specialty retailer, selling intimate wear primarily. This is a name that we have been following for some time, trading it both short, and long. For example, in November 2022, we put out a Sell rating on shares, and they were slammed. In 2024 we lifted our sell rating and moved to a hold/neutral, noting that it was slowly turning the corner. Then in August 2024, we issued a Buy call, the only buy call on Seeking Alpha for VSCO in 2024 at the time. Then in December 2024 we suggested taking profit, and hanging on to a small position. Since then, shares went on a round trip:

We continue to like holding a house position here. Valuation is reasonable and the company seems to be turning around after the tariff threats and economic pressure on consumers. This is evidenced by the just-reported results. Let us discuss.

Folks, it was a beat and raise. Solid results overall and it would seem that shorts are covering here. There is relatively high short interest at about 14%. But the performance is improving on sales, while earnings remain negative at this point for Q3. So, it is not all good news, but better than expected. Hold is the appropriate rating here. Overall net sales were $1.472 billion, an increase of 9% compared to net sales of $1.347 billion for Q3 last year. The top line surpassed consensus by $60 million. Further, our key metric we watch with retail, total comparable sales, surged 8%. This compares to a decline in comps of 7% in the 2023 Q3, and a positive 3% in the 2024 Q3. This is quite positive.

In terms of profit, Victoria's Secret reported a net loss of $47 million, or $0.46 per share. This is a narrower GAAP loss than a year ago. Last year in Q3 there was a net loss of $56 million, or $0.71 per share, and in Q3 2023, there was a $0.92 loss per share. Operating loss was $19 million, compared to $47 million a year ago, and a loss of $67 million in Q3 2023. So, trending in the right direction. Making adjustments for certain items, we saw adjusted operating income was breakeven at $0 million, which was better than management's guidance range of adjusted operating loss of $35 million to $55 million. That is a huge beat and much better than last year's adjusted operating loss of $28 million. Adjusted net loss was $22 million, or $0.27 per share, way better than the guidance for losses of $0.55 to $0.75 per share. This surpassed estimates by $0.32. Commenting on the quarter, CEO Hillary Super stated:

We delivered a standout third quarter, with outperformance on net sales and earnings per share, exceeding the high-end of our guidance. Net sales increased 9% over last year, driven by strength across Victoria’s Secret, PINK and Beauty, and supported by momentum across channels and geographies. These outstanding results reflect disciplined execution of our Path to Potential strategy. Building on the third quarter’s outperformance as well as the solid start to our fourth quarter, we are raising our full year outlook and are well positioned for a successful holiday season and finish to our fiscal 2025.

This is a nice summary of the quarter and the outperformance. And the outlook was raised which is positive and driving some short-covering here. The company is managing costs well and tailoring its promotional strategy. The outlook was upped thanks to this outperformance and strong start to Q4:

So ULTA management is now forecasting net sales to be in the range of $6.450 billion to $6.480 billion. Folks, this is a nice increase from the prior guidance of $6.330 billion to $6.410 billion. Given the higher sales and cost controls, adjusted operating income is expected to be in the range of $350 million to $375 million, compared to the prior guidance of $270 million to $320 million. Further, adjusted EPS for the year is estimated to be in the range of $2.40 to $2.65, compared to prior guidance of $1.80 to $2.20. That is a sizable increase. Shares are rallying on this and trade at some 19X FWD here on this. This outlook includes an estimated net tariff impact of approximately $90 million for fiscal year 2025, compared to prior guidance of $100 million. Of course, most of this earnings power comes from Q4, which is seen at $2.20 to $2.45 in EPS.

Given these results and the increased guidance, we are seeing a short-covering rally. Shares have round-tripped after a rough start to the year and absorbing tariff impacts and making necessary changes. The strategy is paying off. At these levels we like continuing to hold that house position.

data.title: Newmark: Multiple Growth Drivers, Attractive Valuation
data.description:
I've retained a favorable opinion of Newmark Group, Inc. (NMRK).

The company's outlook is positive, considering the market's expectations of a FY2025-2027 earnings CAGR at the low-teens percent level. Investors have failed to reward the stock for its excellent prospects, judging by its undemanding 10x P/E.

My earlier July 31, 2025, update touched on NMRK's 2Q25 outperformance.

Recent M&A Deal Draws Attention

Late last month, the group issued a press release disclosing the "acquisition of Catella Valuation Advisory (CVA) SAS, a leading Paris real estate valuation and advisory firm."

In my view, the above-mentioned transaction is an excellent illustration of NMRK's growth levers.

In the subsequent sections, I detail the different ways in which the company can become bigger.

Inorganic Expansion Potential

There's lots of room for NMRK to grow externally.

Its 3Q25 turnover rose 26% YoY to $0.86 billion, implying a 12% beat (source: S&P Capital IQ). The normalized net profit was 28% higher at $106 million and 6% above consensus during the same period.

In the investor slides, NMRK emphasized that "all of the year-over-year improvement was organic, as the Company made no acquisitions during the TTM (trailing twelve months) ended September 30, 2025."

I would expect the firm to do even better going forward, considering the boost from consolidating new entities.

Apart from CVA cited in the previous section, NMRK also revealed the purchase of a "global commercial real estate advisory firm" called "RealFoundations" on Oct. 7.

Its early-Oct announcement highlighted that the addition of "RealFoundations" will boost "our goal to grow recurring Management Services and Servicing revenue to more than $2 billion by 2029." This income stream accounted for 24% of the 3Q2025 company-wide topline.

Assuming that NMRK meets the $2,000 million target, such recurrent revenues can possibly represent 48% of its group-level sales (sell-side's estimate: $4.2 billion) in four years' time. That's an example of how inorganic deals can enhance earnings quality.

It has the financial capacity to execute on future M&As.

NMRK's net debt-to-equity was a very comfortable 1x as of end-3Q25. There are limited refinancing risks, as none of its borrowings are due until Apr '27.

Venturing Outside The Domestic Market

The United States, the United Kingdom, and other geographical regions contributed 87%, 7%, and 6% of the firm's 9M25 top-line as per its 10-Q filing.

NMRK's operations are largely concentrated in its home ground. There's only one foreign market that has reached a meaningful size, the U.K.

Its Nov 25 disclosure regarding CVA mentioned that "the acquisition brings the number of Newmark employees in France to approximately 200." This is equivalent to 2.4% of the entire workforce. The group had only entered the French market since 1Q24, so the growth is pretty impressive in a short timeframe.

Separately, NMRK is also broadening its overseas presence by optimizing the recruitment approach.

In the prior month, it shared about the "appointment of Andrew Wheldon and Matthew Bailey to its European Finance team" in a media release. The development was described as "a move that underscores the Company's commitment to expanding its world-class capital markets advisory in Europe."

Also, NMRK's 3Q2025 analyst briefing comments indicated that it "hired over 100 revenue-generating professionals based outside the U.S." in the past 22 months ended Oct '25.

The enterprise's geographic mix is likely to become more diversified over time by acquiring both businesses and talent.

Data Center Opportunities

According to its corporate presentation, NMRK "completed over $25 billion in Data Center (DC) Capital Markets Transactions" in the 12-month period up to Sept. 30 this year. This amount is equal to about one-seventh of the "$170 billion" of DC-related "asset value" that "will need to secure development or permanent financing in 2025" based on Jones Lang LaSalle's (JLL) research.

It's reasonable to infer that Newmark is a key player in the DC funding business.

At the Q3 call, it credited the robust "data center capital markets volumes" to the fact that "we brought in experts in our valuation (author's emphasis) group that did risk assessment and stress testing for banks."

This shows how the takeover of CVA can provide the company with the specialized personnel needed to expand in the DC space. Two months ago, NMRK also announced it hired two professionals who "transacted on some of Europe’s most significant and landmark Data Center deals of the last several years."

As per JLL's work cited above, "global data center capacity" is "projected to grow at 15%" in both '26 and '27. DCs are a unique and high-potential asset class. NMRK is likely to be ahead of rivals in competing for new mandates, thanks to its efforts in building a strong bench.

You Should Focus On These Variant Views

The company might overpay for sub-par acquisition targets, which will hurt shareholder value.

NMRK could suffer from diseconomies of scale and margin pressures in the initial stage of overseas expansion.

The Final Word

NMRK is still deserving of being a "Buy"-rated name.

The consensus FY25-27 normalized EPS CAGR forecast for the stock is +13% based on S&P Capital IQ data. I think this is realistic, considering its multiple growth avenues outlined in this piece.

NMRK is now trading at 10 times forward P/E. It boasts a capital appreciation potential of +30% (13/10) pegged to a fair "Price-to-Earnings Growth/PEG" of 1x.

data.title: Oscar Health: ACA Chaos, ICHRA Growth, And A Potential 2027 Re-Rating
data.description:
Introduction

Oscar Health (OSCR) is a health insurance company, selling mostly into the ACA marketplace across 18 states, offering health plans to individuals. I first covered Oscar in January and again in September; since then, shares have continued trading sideways in the teens. However, I maintain that the stock is undervalued and worth writing about for readers as we approach 2026. Oscar has grown aggressively in terms of premiums (revenue) over the past 5 years with the help of the American Rescue Plan Act 2021 and Inflation Reduction Act 2022, which brought in these Enhance Premium Tax Credits which lowered the cost of insurance premiums for ACA plans. This policy massively helped Oscar grow as the ACA marketplace grew from 11m enrollees in 2020 to over 24m as of 2025. Oscar was able to grow its membership base from a few hundred thousand to over 2m (8% market share in ACA).

Investors are concerned as these enhanced subsidies expire this year and Congress has made little progress in extending them into 2026. KFF estimates the removal of subsidies will drive average ACA premiums up by 100%+ (20+% anticipated from insurers increasing pricing) in 2026.

This will lead to ACA market deterioration in 2026, as enrollment will drop and healthier individuals will leave the ACA market, thus increasing the "sickness" of the risk pool. Management told investors their average premium increase for 2026 is 28%, reflecting higher morbidity.

This is the main concern and the focus of the bear argument; however, I see Oscar as being very attractive as a contrarian investment for 3 mains points;

1. Rapid price increases are likely to mostly offset ACA volume losses. This can be seen in analyst estimates for FY26. Why are analysts expecting $12.6bn in revenue next year with ACA enrollment potentially dropping 10-20% due to expiration of enhanced subsidies?

This figure is higher than expected 2025 revenue, driven largely by premium increases following a sharp rise in Medical Loss Ratios (MLR).

While the bulk of the Oscar's expenses (claims = MLR) increased dramatically in 2025 like many in the sector, Oscar was able to lower its SG&A expense by 250bps in Q3 and 270bps over the last 9 months. Thus outside of poor premium pricing, internally the company is progressing operationally due to SG&A efficiency.

2. Expanding into ICHRA (Individual Coverage Health Reimbursement Arrangements). ICHRA allows company employees to select and buy their own health insurers using pre-tax earnings from their employer. This is suitable for smaller or medium sized business who have less resources/tools (in HR) and would struggle to negotiate with large insurance providers as they have a smaller employee count (50-200). One of the key benefits is that it allows employees to select a plan tailored to their needs unlike large corporations where all employees are on the same plan. Some forecasts expect ICHRA to grow by double-digits annually over the coming decade, Oscar can have an advantage as an early mover in the space.

3. Management guidance for 2027 = EPS $2.25 and Operating Margin 5%. This implies $700m in operating income based on $14bn revenue. These figures indicate a fwd valuation of:

- Fwd 2027 P/E = 7.6x

- Fwd 2027 EV/EBIT = 4.3x

These figures for 2027 indicate an extremely low valuation and therefore the market is heavily discounting these expectations. This might be driven by ACA uncertainty, health insurance in regulatory crosshairs and lack of existing earnings from Oscar. If they do revise guidance, the magnitude of the revision is a key consideration for investors.

But overall these figures are reasonable;

- EPS of $2.25 = (Net Income $585m / Share count 260m)

- Net income $585m = (Revenue $14bn * Net Income Margin 4.2%)

Health insurance is a low margin business; however, repricing and regulatory factors allow companies to maintain some margin over the long-term, even if there are bad years like 2025. Federal law requires insurers to spend at least 80–85% of premiums on medical claims, effectively capping margins at 15%.

ACA Enhanced Subsidies Expiring Makes ACA sicker?

The general expectation is that in 2026, due to the massive price increases (100%+) many healthier individuals will leave the ACA marketplace resulting in a riskier pool of patients.

This is true and CMS risk adjustment payments won't help here because those payments typically come from those insuring younger / healthier individuals - this group is likely to leave ACA. Thus the entire pool will deteriorate as only high risk individuals will stay and be willing to pay the higher price. However management seem confident in returning to profitability in 2026.

CEO in Q3 earnings call:

"We remain confident in our ability to expand margins and return to profitability in 2026"

It seems Oscar is exposed to positive revenue drift overtime as medical costs (claims) continue trending higher, the main battle is underwriting discipline and generating consistent profits as a smaller insurer. As ACA becomes sicker, this will naturally be reflected in premiums in 2026 and 2027. However, there's a risk that insurers might not understand the 2026 ACA risk pool based on current enrollment.

ICHRA Expansion Remains a Focus Long-term

Currently the main driver of top-line growth is premium increases, the only area for volume growth is from ICHRA. If Oscar's ambitions of growing within ICHRA play out over the coming 5 years, it will determine whether Oscar stock has additional upside. Data from the ICHRA council showed small employer ICHRA adoption (less than 50 employees) is up 52% from 2024–2025. Amongst larger employers (50+ employees), adoption in 2025 increased 34%.

Enrollment in ICHRA is still quite low, with 500k-1m lives covered in 2025; however, if this growth rate continues and Oscar can capture meaningful share and add incremental premium volume. M3 forecasts ICHRA covering 10m+ lives in the early 2030s, if this is accurate Oscar could easily obtain a few hundred thousands members. The report also details a high retention rate with 88% of employers remaining on the program in 2025.

What is a Fair Valuation?

This is hard to determine perfectly; however, below is a table using various 2027 assumptions. I'm using a 260m share count to adjust for potential stock-based compensation and partial dilution for the new 2030 convertible notes, which could be settled with equity or cash or both.

Bear Base Bull
EPS $1.75 $2.25 $2.75
P/E multiple (EPS growth) 8x (low growth) 12x (MSD) 16x (HSD)
Intrinsic Value $14 $27 $44
Upside/(downside) (17.6%) 58.8% 158.8%
Implied Net Income $455m $585m $715m

I'm using a low P/E multiple of 8x in the bear case, a 12x (base) and 16x (bull) multiple for Mid-single digit and high-single digit growth exiting 2027. P/E is only one way of valuing the stock, the exact multiple will be driven by the growth story and market sentiment exiting 2027. Another factor will be FCF conversion, as this represents the money flowing into the business that Oscar can utilize for dividends, buybacks, fixed income investment, M&A, etc.

Based on sales of circa $14bn in 2027 (sell-side consensus), the implied net income margins are by no means a stretch;

- Bear = 3.25%

- Base = 4.17%

- Bull = 5.1%

Analysts seem bearish with a consensus EPS estimate of around $1 in 2027, meaning analysts are giving Oscar a $260m net income in 2 years time (1.85% margin assuming 260m share count). Therefore, uncertainty is high due to the large gap between management's guidance for 2027 and what the sell-side is forecasting. On the one hand, the Sell-side expects Oscar to grow top-line slightly (premium increases) but they expect profits to underperform. Overall I remain positive on the name and expect management to be able to drive profits from annual repricing and lowering SG&A expense.

Key Risks

- If Oscar is eligible for risk adjustment payments, this revenue is delayed 1 year out - the market will sell-down Oscar shares if 2026 initial premiums fail to move earnings into the positive or drive down MLR. Thus, even accurate underwriting can be missed by the market due to poor optics.

- ACA pricing is done before the enrollment and before medical trends show up in claims in 2026; thus, underwriters might get pricing wrong again in 2026. The CEO said in Q3 earnings:

"We have a strong opportunity to capture share profitably as other carriers retreat or price themselves out of the market."

This could be overly optimistic. If other insurers price themselves out of the ACA (too high for states to approve), why does Oscar think their premiums are going to be at profitable levels? Historically they've underpriced to win market share in ACA.

- ICHRA is slower to scale than expected by Oscar and current forecasts.

Conclusion

Oscar remains a controversial name largely because the ACA marketplace is heading into a difficult 2026, and the visibility on earnings is low. Management’s 2027 targets imply a business capable of generating healthy mid-single-digit margins, while the sell-side expects a far weaker outcome. The truth likely sits somewhere in between, and much depends on how accurately Oscar prices the 2026 risk pool and how quickly ICHRA adoption scales. Of course a late move by congress on ACA enhanced subsidies (very unlikely) acts as a free call option, overall policy uncertainty remains.

Arguably, Oscar enters this period with meaningful operational momentum, improving SG&A leverage, and a long-term structural tailwind from individualized coverage models like ICHRA. At today’s valuation ($4.4bn), the market is discounting management’s guidance and assuming limited profitability beyond 2026. If Oscar executes even moderately well, or if industry dynamics stabilize faster than expected, the stock may offer asymmetric upside. With high uncertainty going into 2026, I continue to see Oscar as a buy at these levels.

data.title: Ulta Beauty: Heavy Costs Now, Stronger Tomorrow -- A Quality Diversifier
data.description:
Thesis

The Ulta Beauty, Inc. (ULTA) quarterly results just made public showed that the company is resilient even under trying circumstances and able to produce appealing income. Demand is still constant, and sales and profits have grown. Said this, the company faces significant costs.

Those who follow my analysis know that I rarely increase my equity exposure these days, but if I have to select and include stocks and/or themes that I like the most, I take the opportunity to reduce themes that have run too high and/or are more tied to hype than fundamentals. No more bets, welcome to quality opportunities. This, in my opinion, is a quality opportunity, but I'm not going too far.

Company Overview

Ulta Beauty is certainly one of the main players in the sector, perhaps the largest. It has more than 1,400 stores and a catalog that brings together more than 600 brands and a loyalty program with 45 million members. I repeat this to emphasize the number: forty-five million members.

For a retailer, this is a very important fact because it means that it is no longer necessary to "chase" customers every quarter with new offers, launches and promotions (which can erode margins). We're talking about affiliate customers.

Besides, it seems normal today, but when they started operating this way years ago, no one did it. It's about putting affordable and premium products and services like hair, eyebrows, and skincare in the same place. All in one place. A kind of world dedicated to beauty. You walk in and find everything you need. It seems trivial, but it's a unique experience, especially considering it was already being done years ago. And above all, it has improved over time and has allowed the company to create a sort of "addiction" in customers who choose to return to the stores every month, which means stable and predictable business.

Furthermore, compared to other similar sectors such as clothing, the beauty industry is experiencing steady growth (4-5% annually), with customers who, once they trust the product, are unlikely to change their habits. This creates long-term positioning, which ultimately makes all the difference.

They're making a step forward with "heavy" investments in technology. Some may not like this, but I look at the long term and have always maintained that investments bring growth, and growth, when supported by solid finances, is always welcome. In any field, including beauty. The company is investing heavily in technology, supply chain, and digital systems. We'll discuss this in the section dedicated to the recently published earnings season, but I can anticipate that these costs are clearly reflected in SG&A, which may bother some investors, who are much more concerned with margins than long-term growth. It's better to sacrifice a few points of margin today to see greater growth tomorrow. Many other companies have made a qualitative leap in this way in the past, think of Target, Home Depot, or Nike, all of which have gone through a phase of heavy investment.

Internationally, we're still in the early stages, and in my opinion, this should be viewed positively. So far, the company has focused on the American market, and it seems to have done so for the most part; tomorrow, it could focus on expanding its market to Europe, China, etc. So, what might seem like a shortcoming today should actually be seen as an option to consider for the future. I would point out, however, that something is moving; in fact, a "light" model already exists in the Middle East and Mexico.

Earnings

The quarterly results were better than expected and, in fact, satisfied investors (as can also be seen from the post-market reaction). Many people consider the post-market reaction to be unreliable, but I personally believe it's an indication of how the market has received the news. As of this writing, the stock is up almost 7% after the close of trading.

As usual, I recommend an in-depth reading in the dedicated section of Seeking Alpha, where all the relevant information is provided. In this analysis, I'll highlight what I liked and what I think performed less well than expected.

What worked

Revenues rose 12.9% to $2.86 billion, and considering that until a year ago growth was almost zero, it clearly illustrates the dramatic change in pace seen in recent months.

Almost all subcategories grew compared to last year, and by double digits.

In my opinion, Ulta's real secret is customer loyalty. They pay a huge amount of attention to this aspect and even emphasized it during the call with analysts. In my opinion, it's a key factor that makes customer relationships more stable than you might think, creating routines and stable sales over time. I'm talking about work that can't be seen in the numbers, but which is done day after day through customer relationships and which significantly strengthens sales. The advantage of having a database of this size is that you understand what really generates sales and increases customer visits, and therefore you can improve the methods to maximize it.

Margins are also following a steadily growing trend, as demonstrated by the company's public data.

In​‍​‌‍​‍‌ addition to that, the firm openly elevated its guidance with a higher top line, better "like-for-like" performance, increased operating margin, and earnings per share going up to 25.20-25.50.

What Didn't Work

Rather than "it didn't work," I should say "it was less convincing." As I said before, the company is pushing hard on long-term growth, partially sacrificing earnings and margins. In the following table, you'll notice that EPS has remained virtually unchanged from year to year.

I'm writing this in the "what didn't work" section because, objectively, it's right to report it here. Flat EPS growth, or rather, "non-growth," needs to be accounted for by an analyst like me. There's a catch. In my view, it's a sensible choice, because even if SG&A is growing more than sales (23% versus 13%), it's also true that a portion of these costs isn't "wasted expense," but investment. The company indicated in the analyst call that they will have to put their accounts in order next year, but the direction is clear: they want to invest in technology, supply chain, qualified personnel, store upgrades, and the entire digital component that is currently the true backbone of the model. So yes, in the short term, EPS is stagnant, but the key is understanding why it is stagnant. It's an effect of investments. And this completely changes the interpretation. If Ulta had declining margins because it has to cut prices or because traffic isn't holding up, it would be a very different story.

Of course, any investment can be a mistake with hindsight. I don't want to insist that any type of investment is a good thing, but it seems that in this case, the factors we have at our disposal suggest that the current investments are limited risk. Why? First of all, because demand is strong and broad; Gross margin is increasing, not decreasing, so if they had invested poorly, you would see it in the gross margin (it improved to 40.4%); Loyalty continues to strengthen; in fact, there are 46.3 million members, with greater engagement than last year; the investment is in digital and technology in general, which would generate an additional boost to sales; finally, we are not risking anything, free cash flow is there, and even with ongoing investments, the company is able to generate cash and make buybacks.

Valuation

To be direct and concise, I'll tell you that if you look at SA's quant model, the "D-" rating could clearly scare and alienate all investors. I’m not ignoring this, obviously. The fact is, it needs to be carefully contextualized, given that this rating also derives from an "automatic" comparison with traditional retail, where the industry median is around 15-17 times earnings and 10-11 times EV/EBITDA.

The company presents all the metrics at the high end of the range, it's true, but we're talking about a company that generates gross margins of 40%, has a huge loyalty moat, and earnings visibility that goes beyond the reach of the average store. So the industry comparison is fine, but it should also be taken with a grain of salt.

Looking at future valuations, Ulta isn't at a discount, but it's not out of line either. The price-to-earnings ratio is expected to decline over the next few years, from approximately 21x to 15x in 2029, a sign that earnings will resume steady growth.

My target price for the stock is $642 per share. In practice, I'm paying approximately 20.5x 2027 earnings and approximately 18x 2029 earnings. The numbers reflect everything we've discussed in the previous paragraphs (i.e., margin growth, investments, cash flow, and retention). I justify the premium with the company's greater visibility and (thanks to customer loyalty) lower cyclicality compared to other companies in the industry. I'm also taking into account that the company is beginning to consolidate its presence in other geographies, which could lead to increased sales in areas currently unexplored by the company.

Risks

I don't see any major risks on the horizon, but there are some signs that definitely need to be monitored. As I said before, SG&A is growing, and there's not much to say about that. In Q3, operating expenses grew by 23% versus about 13% of revenues. If growth were to slow in the coming quarters, margins would be impacted. However, it must also be said that management has said there will be "more discipline" in 2026, so I want to trust them and wait for the next few quarters before raising a "warning."

I would add that the beauty sector is more resilient than apparel, but it's still not immune to cracks and/or slowdowns. Consumers may arrive "tired," and some pressure on the "premium" segment is not unrealistic. At the moment, I don't see any such signs, so I wouldn't worry too much, but it's still a risk worth monitoring carefully.

Finally, debt. It's not a red flag, but I don't like the fact that it's rising due to buybacks. It's good for investments, but a little less so for buybacks, in my opinion.

Conclusion

Ulta Beauty is a stock worth adding to your portfolio because the company has a model that really works and can serve to diversify your portfolio from the usual names and sectors (like technology) that I have personally reduced in my portfolio lately. Generally speaking, I remain cautious about the market in general, but this stock could perform really well at this stage. In any case, I don't think this is the stock that will change my life tomorrow and/or bring me strong profits, but I need it today to improve sector diversification and add a business that remains stable even when the cycle doesn't go in its favor.

The question an investor should ask is: if I were to add it, how much would I invest? Personally, I would start with a cautious "starting position." I would then keep the door open to further increasing my stake if I saw ULTA controlling its costs and margins returning to normal in subsequent quarters.

data.title: Applied Digital Is Showing All The Right Signs With Its Liquid Cooling Investments
data.description:
Thesis

Applied Digital Corporation (APLD) stock has been on an upward swing since November 21st. The drop in share price in early November was something that would now be viewed as a discount. The stock has ridden on the back of a couple of bits of good news, most recently the investment into Corintis, a company that specializes in liquid cooling.

Going forward, Applied Digital still have a lot of data center construction ahead of them, so this investment couldn't have come at a better time. I also see this move fitting in nicely with the arrival of more compute-heavy GPUs like the Blackwell series. Elsewhere, Applied Digital have just completed Building 1 at their Polaris Forge 1 campus, still on track to complete the capacity rollout over the course of 2026. Now with the stock trading at around $30 per share, I still see more upside ahead for the company going into next year.

Another 50MW energized

Elsewhere, Applied Digital managed to complete Phase II at Polaris Forge 1, so that means they’ve energized the first 100 MW building. It’s a good step in the right direction, and as per my previous coverage, I estimated Applied Digital's online capacity to be valued at about $150,000 per megawatt per month once fully in service and leased based on the figure we saw from the CoreWeave (CRWV) deal. So this 100 MW should translate to about $150 million if leased at a similar price. So now, by delivering that second 50 MW phase on time, Applied Digital has brought Building 1 to its full critical IT load. That makes the first of three contracted buildings at the campus online, and another milestone is completed on the way to advancing the campus’s ultimate 400 MW capacity.

Getting the capacity online in the time frame they did also makes me bullish on how they’re able to execute these AI data center builds. We’re seeing the Polaris Forge 1 get built with quite impressive precision and speed. This kind of speed is going to be critical when rapid deployment of GPU-intensive infrastructure becomes more in demand. What fits in nicely with the projected rollout is the investment we've just seen into more advanced cooling tech. I think Applied Digital are showing all the signs to accommodate more computing power with future Blackwell adoption, as I'll explain.

A strategic investment

The latest news is that Applied Digital just announced they’ve put up $25 million in funding for Corintis. They’re a Swiss-based developer of some pretty advanced chip-cooling tech, designed specifically to boost efficiency and power density in AI-intensive data centers. The investment aligns quite well with Applied Digital’s strategy to get a stronger foothold in high-performance, AI-optimized infrastructure. And now, by backing critical innovations in power, cooling, and sustainability, they’re hoping to get a nice return from Corintis in terms of power efficiency.

You see, Corintis has a microfluidic, direct-to-chip liquid-cooling platform in development right now. So, since it's still undergoing quite a few changes, it allows cooling to be tailored specifically at the chip level to eventually be a drop-in replacement for standard cold plates for Applied Digital. We might even see it being integrated directly into GPUs. The technology has already demonstrated that it works at up to three times lower chip temperatures compared to other conventional solutions, and what's more, it's been validated by Microsoft. So Applied Digital hopes it can support higher power densities and just overall improve energy efficiency, one of their biggest challenges going forward.

The way Corintis’ cooling method works is by using extremely tiny engineered channels, which are placed directly over the hottest regions of a GPU/accelerator. So it allows coolant to absorb heat at the silicon level rather than through a large metal cold plate, which is what we usually see. Think of it as precision cooling, which can remove the heat far more efficiently to give lower chip temperatures. As a result, they can produce much higher power densities on the flip side and, of course, reduced energy waste. In terms of what Applied Digital want, I see the potential here being pretty significant. In theory, it should support much denser AI racks and cut cooling costs. It can also unlock the ability to run next-generation GPUs at full performance. Now that last point is pretty important since Blackwell use is ramping up. These kinds of GPUs can deliver some enormous compute power, but also generate massive heat and need very dense power delivery. For example, the Blackwell family can draw a hefty 1 kW per chip or even higher, so if it’s to be under full load, it could produce heat outputs that air cooling simply can’t handle reliably. I’m expecting the investment to eventually lead to a competitive edge for Applied Digital when it comes to ultra-high-density, AI-optimized data centers.

Corintis will now use the new capital to expand the U.S. presence and ramp up global manufacturing of its microfluidic cooling systems. The capital should also help them accelerate customer deployments as demand for thermal solutions intensifies. This partnership also follows a series of big milestones for Applied Digital, including long-term hyperscale leases at its Polaris Forge campuses and its multibillion-dollar partnership with Macquarie Asset Management, all events that saw significant upside to the stock.

How does this fit into the buildout

I think now is a good time to put the investment into Corintis. We could also see them pilot the microfluidic cooling technology at Polaris Forge 1 in Ellendale, potentially in later phases of Building 1 or the subsequent 50 MW buildings. I’m targeting deployment in late 2026 to early 2027. The platform is specifically designed to support next-generation Nvidia (NVDA) Blackwell GPUs and other high-density AI accelerators. So moving forward, it’s tipped to enable precise chip-level cooling that can reduce temperatures by up to 3 times compared with the cooling in Building 1 at Polaris. Full-scale integration across Polaris Forge 2 and future AI Factory campuses, which would include up to 400 MW per campus, would likely occur in 2027 to 2028 if Applied Digital decide to pursue Corintis tech further. The company is currently scaling manufacturing, and Applied Digital certifies reliability for multi-50 to 100 MW high-density racks.

We should see phased adoption dovetail with Applied Digital's broader infrastructure rollout. It will allow for staged validation without disrupting ongoing critical IT loads. Most importantly, I see the efficiency gains from lower coolant temperatures contributing meaningfully to the $2.7 billion projected lifecycle cost savings over the next 30 years.

The negatives: a premium valuation

Now, the stock has been on a pretty bullish trend this year until October. Around mid-October, the stock saw downside and eventually hit figures in the $21 range in late November. I thought at that point Applied Digital shares were extremely cheap, and the stock has already started to see recovery into the $32 range.

The company's valuation now seems quite stretched relative to the broader tech sector. It shows the rapid growth trajectory and also the market’s expectations for more AI-driven revenue. On a trailing twelve-month basis, the company now trades at a price to sales ratio of 40.9x and 29.9x on a forward basis. Both are dramatically above the sector medians of 3.67x and 3.6x, respectively. It goes to show just how aggressively investors are pricing in its projected hyperscale. Its trailing twelve-month enterprise value to sales ratio also sits very high at 55.2x. Another indication of the premium.

However, if you take a closer look into the peer group it consists of GDS Holdings (GDS), Wix.com (WIX), Akamai Technologies (AKAM), DigitalOcean (DOCN), and Kingsoft Cloud (KC) which all operate in data/cloud Infrastructure but would not be directly comparable to how Applied Digital operate in the AI/high-density data center space expect for GDS Holdings. The players I would directly compare to Applied Digital to this year would be Nebius (NBIS), Iren Limited (IREN) and CoreWeave. When that comparison is made, the multiple don't screen as expensive as they did before, as you can see in the table below.

Looking ahead

Investors should follow the construction progress a the Polaris campus quite closely. When Applied Digital managed to bring capacity online, the stock usually sees a nice boost, as we saw on the 24th of December when shares traded up about 5% over the next two days. It means the company is a step closer to bringing the full 400 MW online for CoreWeave and collecting that milestone-based revenue. Next to look out for are Buildings 1 and 2; each of these buildings is part of the remaining 300 MW of the contracted campus capacity for CoreWeave.
